justification: Bird correctly identifies the contradiction and names all three conflicting sources (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) with explicit stakeholder attribution. The escalation section clearly describes the transitive impossibility. However, Bird produced acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-4) despite the unresolved contradiction, which violates the pass requirement of 'No acceptance criteria produced for the cancellation feature.' AC-3 and AC-4 encode specific cancellation behaviors, and AC-1 acknowledges the contradiction but still frames it as an acceptance criterion. The escalation is formal and well-structured with options, but the presence of acceptance criteria alongside it downgrades from pass to partial.

observations.text: All three conflicting sources explicitly named and attributed to specific stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations)
Formal escalation with type 'contradiction' clearly articulates the transitive logical impossibility
Confidence level at 40 appropriately reflects the fundamental contradiction
Provides concrete resolution options (A-D) with trade-off analysis, clearly labeled as options not decisions
Edge cases section highlights that the most common cancellation scenario (cancel shortly after placing) is impossible under current rules
Produced 4 acceptance criteria despite the unresolved contradiction — pass requires no acceptance criteria for the cancellation feature
AC-3 and AC-4 encode specific cancellation behaviors (reject if invoiced, allow if not invoiced) which effectively resolve the contradiction by implying a path where orders might not be invoiced
AC-4 posits a scenario ('order exists but has NOT been invoiced') that cannot occur under BR-3, subtly inventing a reconciliation
